-- 1 of 5 --

Timelines for Your Internal Launch
!
Okay, so now I want to talk about the timing of the pre-launch.
!
Lots and lots of questions about this, I always get lots of questions. It’s only natural
because you see the sequences and want to know how they all fit together.
!
To be clear, this is not to scale. I’m going to split it. It’s basically three main periods
here.
!
In the middle we have the pre-launch. You’ve heard me talk about pre-launch. That’s
the sideways sales letter where you release your three pieces of pre-launch content,
you’ve got people all excited about your launch. That’s the pre-launch.
!
Then we come into the open cart. That’s where you actually open up and start taking
orders, that's the open cart.
!
We have a pre-launch period and open cart period.
!
The one you haven’t heard me talk too much about is what we call the pre-prelaunch.
!
The pre-prelaunch could be the longest of all these, so the pre-pre-launch can last
anywhere from 1 to 4 weeks long.
!
They’re usually about 2 weeks long for me. They’re a lot less defined than these 2
periods. I’m actually going to be talking about the pre-prelaunch in this module and
walking you through some examples.
!
A pre-prelaunch can be very, very cool. It’s something you don’t have to do, but once
you start using some type of a pre-prelaunch then you’ll realize how cool they are and
then you’ll start to think about them more often.
!
Then the pre-launch period is typically 9 to 12 days long in most launches. It could be
longer, could be shorter.
!
Now typically bigger-ticket items, or if you come out with a brand new flagship product,
a really big important launch for you, it’s generally going to be longer.
!
If it’s more of a quicker, a lower-priced product for you, then it could be shorter. Of
course, pricing is completely relative to whatever market you’re in.
!
If you’re teaching Fortune 500 executives how to present themselves on stage, okay,
that’s probably a really high-ticket item, high-dollar item. If you’re teaching people how
to decorate cakes, probably not going to be as high-priced.
When I’m talking about whether something’s high-priced or low-priced, it’s relative to
your market. I don’t know what cake decorating products go for, but maybe at the low
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 2
PLF_Module1v1.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 2 of 5 --

end they’re $10, at the high end maybe they’re $50.
The thing is, with those executives, teaching those Fortune 500 executives how to
present, you’re probably talking more like $2000 might be the low end and $10,000
might be the high end. Again, when I’m talking about higher-ticket items and lower-
ticket items, it’s relative to your market.
!
You never have to apologize to me if you have a lower-end market. I’ve got plenty of
PLF owners who sell lots and lots of products that are not $1000,$2000 or even $5000.
They might be selling $10 products or $100 products and selling tons of them and
they’ve got a great business. Don’t ever apologize to me or anyone else about your
pricing. The key is, is I think your using these type of strategies, you should be at the
high end of whatever your market is so it’s completely relative to what other people are
selling in your market.
!
In any case, typically a higher-ticket item’s going to have a longer pre-launch and a
lower-ticket item’s going to have a shorter pre-launch. You need a lot less copy, a lot
less marketing to sell a gallon of milk compared to selling a Lamborghini, right? It just
makes sense. A higher-ticket sale is a more involved sale, generally. It needs more
education and just more communication.
!
Most pre-launch periods, 9 to 12 days and then the open cart period is
typically 5 to 7 days.
!
Now you can go shorter. I’ve had open cart periods for my launches that were like a
day-and-a-half, okay? The thing is, if you’re going to go really short, probably not a
good thing to do on your very first launch because if something isn't perfect, it doesn’t
give you time to recover. A longer cart period gives you more time just to tweak your
message and recover from any mistakes you make during your open cart.
!
Remember there’s that product launch parachute. The product launch parachute
means that there’s lots and lots of interactions throughout this entire process so if
something isn’t perfect, you have time to recover.
!
You’re not depending on one email or one video or one anything. Those really short
open-cart periods, they’re a little on the dangerous side and when I did them, well, I’m
a trained professional. I’ve done dozens and dozens and dozens of these launches so I
felt comfortable doing it.
!
Now on the other hand, go beyond 7 days and it will often turn into a beg-a-thon,
because when your cart is open, you’re going to want to be emailing your launch list
every single day.
Frankly, after 7 days you start to run out of things to say for most launches. Later on I’ll
get into what you actually do during that open cart period.
!

-- 3 of 5 --

I think for most people, 5 to 7 days is that golden period. Let’s just blow this out a little
bit...
!
Typically on day 1 of your prelaunch period is your first piece of PLC. This is just classic.
All this stuff’s very, very flexible and you can change stuff around.
!
PLC number 2 would typically be on day 4. You release your pre-launch content then on
day 1. Then day 2, day 3, you let people digest it. Then day 4 will be your second piece
of pre-launch content, and then on day 8, will be PLC number 3.
!
Then you’ll open cart on day 10. This is day 10 and you’re going to open. This can vary
quite a bit.
!
You can go day 1, you could go day 3, you could go day 7, you could go day 9, no
problem, but that’s typically how it will go.
!
The key here is you want to give your people a little bit of time to digest each piece of
content as you move forward. That’s just general timing, and then of course from the
open cart you go forward 5 days.
!
From the start of pre-launch to when you close cart will typically be anywhere from 14
to 19 days. In other words, if you wanted the short end here, you want 9 days pre-
launch and 5 days open cart, that would give you 14 days from the start of pre-launch
to the end, to the closing cart.
!
If you wanted a 12-day pre-launch and you kept the cart open for 7 days, that’s 19
days from the start of pre-launch to the end of the launch when you close the cart.
!
When I say close cart, that’s one little asterisk. We’ll get to this in more detail later on,
but when I say close cart, that means you’re closing your launch offer. At the end of
every launch, there has to be some negative consequence if people don’t buy from you.
!
One silly way I put it is something bad has to happen if they don’t take action, but that
doesn’t mean you have to pull the product off the market.
!
Lots of times I do pull the product off the market because of the nature of the way I
teach Product Launch Formula. Just because I do that doesn’t mean you have to do it.
!
There’s lots of other ways to basically put that definitive end on your launch...
! • 	you can close the cart, meaning that people can’t buy from you
• 	you can raise the price
• 	bonuses can go away
!
There’s different ways to do it, but you have to put that definitive end on your launch or
else there’s going to be a lot of people that are sitting on the fence that don’t take
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 4

-- 4 of 5 --

action unless you hold their feet to the fire and say, “This is when you have to take
action.”
!
That’s the timing of the overall pre-launch, 1 to 4 weeks long, 9 to 12 days, and 5 to 7
days, and now we’re going to get into the pre-prelaunch.
